coredata.dc:title: Rapid and simple nanosensor by combination of graphene quantum dots and enzymatic inhibition mechanisms
coredata.dc:creator: Caballero-Díaz, E.
Benítez-Martínez, S.
Valcárcel, M.
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, we report a simple, rapid and reproducible sensing approach for determination of fenoxycarb pesticide in river water based on the combined use of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and acetylcholinesterase (AChE) enzyme as biorecognition element. Nanoparticles were used as fluorescent probes, while AChE was employed for inhibitor screening. The enzyme-generated products quenched the native fluorescence of N-GQDs. When fenoxycarb was introduced into the assay solution, the activity of AChE to convert substrate into products resulted to be reduced and consequently, N-GQD fluorescence was recovered gradually in an inhibitor concentration-dependent manner. Preliminary studies showed that nanoparticles were not directly sensitive to fenoxycarb, however, the inclusion of an enzymatic system in solution allowed for successful determination of pesticide in aqueous samples. Under optimized conditions, fenoxycarb concentrations ranging from 6 to 70μM exhibited an excellent linear relationship with the inhibition efficiency percentage (R 2 =0.9941). The proposed biosensor has a limit of detection (LOD) of 3.15μM, and excellent reproducibility values (RSD <3%) in river water analysis. Additionally, this analytical strategy is rapid and simple, and it provides a promising alternative for assaying AChE activity and screening of its inhibitors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped graphene quantum dots
Fluorescent sensor
Enzymatic inhibition
Fenoxycarb
Acetylcholinesterase

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure body mmlmath acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)
Influence of AChE and ATC concentrations
Effect of pH of ATC solution
Study of pH and dilution of N-GQD synthesis solution
Determination of fenoxycarb in river water

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Principle of the proposed biosensor based on N-GQDs.
N-GQDs characterization. (a) HR-TEM images of N-GQDs and (b) MIR spectra of undoped and doped GQDs.
(a) Survey XPS spectra of N-GQDs. (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s and (d) O 1s spectra of the N-GQDs.

Fluorescence spectra corresponding to N-GQDs single, in presence of 8.09mM ATC, and in presence of 8.09mM ATC+13.7UmL−1 AChE. Excitation and emission wavelengths were 355nm and 440nm, respectively. Slit width was set at 2nm.

Influence of AChE (a, b) and ATC (c, d) concentrations on the N-GQD fluorescence quenching. The AChE concentrations studied were in the range 0–27.4UmL−1 for 8.09mM ATC and 10-fold dilution of N-GQD synthesis solution. For the study of ATC, concentrations ranging from 0 to 25.3mM were tested for 27.4UmL−1 AChE and 10-fold dilution of N-GQD synthesis solution. FO and F correspond to the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs in absence and presence of AChE (a, b) or ATC (c, d). Excitation and emission wavelengths were 355nm and 440nm, respectively. Slit width was set at 2nm. Data are presented as the mean value and standard deviation obtained for three independent experiments.

Impact of pH (a) and dilution (b) of N-GQD synthesis solution on fluorescence quenching. Experimental conditions were: 27.4UmL−1 AChE, 14.16mM ATC and 10-fold (a) or different dilutions (b) of N-GQD synthesis solution. FO and F correspond to the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs+ATC and N-GQDs+ATC+AChE, respectively. Excitation and emission wavelengths were 355nm and 440nm, respectively. Slit width was set at 2nm. Data are presented as the mean value and standard deviation obtained for three independent experiments.
Studies of (a) incubation time of AChE (E)+ATC (S) and (b) incubation time of AChE (E)+ATC (S)+N-GQDs.

(a) Linear correlation between inhibition efficiency percentage and the concentrations of fenoxycarb in ultrapure water. Experimental conditions were: 27.4UmL−1 AChE, 14.16mM ATC, 10-fold dilution of N-GQD synthesis solution and increasing concentrations of fenoxycarb (6–70μM). Excitation and emission wavelengths were 355nm and 440nm, respectively. Slit width was set at 3.5nm. Data are presented as the mean value and standard deviation obtained for three independent experiments. (b) Fluorescence spectra obtained from the analysis of different fenoxycarb concentrations levels in ultrapure water.

Fluorescence spectra from analysis of spiked river water samples. Experimental conditions were: 27.4UmL−1 AChE, 14.16mM ATC, 10-fold dilution of N-GQD synthesis solution and 20, 40 and 60μM fenoxycarb. Excitation and emission wavelengths were 355nm and 440nm, respectively. Slit width was set at 3.5nm. Recoveries for each concentration level were 57.9–66.4%, 65.9–69.3% and 74.2–77.1% respectively.

head.ce:title: Rapid and simple nanosensor by combination of graphene quantum dots and enzymatic inhibition mechanisms

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, University of Córdoba, E-14071 Córdoba, Spain

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Both authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, we report a simple, rapid and reproducible sensing approach for determination of fenoxycarb pesticide in river water based on the combined use of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and acetylcholinesterase (AChE) enzyme as biorecognition element. Nanoparticles were used as fluorescent probes, while AChE was employed for inhibitor screening. The enzyme-generated products quenched the native fluorescence of N-GQDs. When fenoxycarb was introduced into the assay solution, the activity of AChE to convert substrate into products resulted to be reduced and consequently, N-GQD fluorescence was recovered gradually in an inhibitor concentration-dependent manner. Preliminary studies showed that nanoparticles were not directly sensitive to fenoxycarb, however, the inclusion of an enzymatic system in solution allowed for successful determination of pesticide in aqueous samples. Under optimized conditions, fenoxycarb concentrations ranging from 6 to 70μM exhibited an excellent linear relationship with the inhibition efficiency percentage (R 2 =0.9941). The proposed biosensor has a limit of detection (LOD) of 3.15μM, and excellent reproducibility values (RSD <3%) in river water analysis. Additionally, this analytical strategy is rapid and simple, and it provides a promising alternative for assaying AChE activity and screening of its inhibitors.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent carbon nanodots have received increasing attention in a wide variety of analytical and biomedical applications owing to their outstanding properties [1]. Among them, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are characterized by a well-defined structure of nanosheets of sp2 carbons and exhibit extraordinary luminescent properties due to quantum confinement, edge effects, oxygen-containing groups, structural defects and doping elements. Moreover, their large surface area, chemical inertness, excellent photostability, water solubility and low cytotoxicity make them appropriate candidates for biosensing purposes [2]. Analytical applications of GQDs for optical and electrochemical sensing platforms have been recently reviewed in literature [3].

Organophosphorus and carbamate pesticides are widely used in agriculture because of their relatively low persistence and high effectiveness for insect eradication [4] As a result of their use and release into the environment, these pesticides might suppose a potential risk for human health and environmental compartments. Particularly, ethyl 2-(4-phenoxyphenoxy)ethylcarbamate, commonly known as fenoxycarb, is a general use pesticide belonging to the chemical group of carbamates that acts as an insect growth regulator with juvenile hormone activity [5]. In spite of being considered as one of the least toxic carbamates, there is some evidence suggesting that it might exert harmful effects to some non-target species, such as aquatic invertebrates [6,7]. As a consequence, there is a strict regulation on its conditions of use and concentration levels in environmental matrices and agricultural products. Up to now, the majority of analytical approaches for determining fenoxycarb are based on a previous extraction procedure followed by analysis using enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [8], capillary electrophoresis [9], high-performance liquid chromatography [10], and primarily, liquid/gas chromatography–tandem mass spectrometry [11–13]. Nevertheless, most of these techniques are time-consuming, expensive and require trained personnel.

Recently, enzyme-based sensors have emerged as simple, inexpensive, selective and sensitive alternatives to the aforementioned techniques [14]. Cholinesterases can be used as recognition elements, principally for assay of their inhibitors. In this line, both organophosphorus and carbamate pesticides are known to inhibit the activity of cholinesterase enzyme, being their mechanism of action quite similar. Specifically, carbamates are pseudoirreversible inhibitors of cholinesterases that act by binding of carbamate moiety to serine hydroxyl in the esteratic part of the active site of enzyme [15]. Some reports have concentrated on the fabrication of cholinesterase-based biosensors with colorimetric/fluorometric readout [16–18]. To the best of our knowledge, there is only two recent studies on literature that take advantage of the outstanding luminescent properties of fluorescent carbon nanodots for the fabrication of sensing systems based on inhibition of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) [2,19].

Herein, we report the application of GQD-based sensing method for determination of fenoxycarb carbamate in surface waters through its inhibitory activity on AChE enzyme. The native fluorescence of synthesized nitrogen doped-GQDs (N-GQDs) was quenched in presence of products of enzymatic hydrolysis of acetylthiocholine chloride (ATC). After addition of inhibitor, the enzymés activity decreased and the fluorescence of N-GQDs was progressively recovered in a fenoxycarb concentration-dependent manner. Thus, an increasing concentration of fenoxycarb led to both a higher percentage of enzymatic inhibition and fluorescence recovery. Given that fenoxycarb does not directly interact with N-GQDs, this study proposes a simple and rapid biosensor for fluorimetric analysis of this pesticide by combination of N-GQDs as fluorescence probes and AChE as biorecognition element.

Acetylcholinesterase (AChE) from Electrophorus electricus (200–1000 units mg−1 protein), acetylthiocholine chloride (≥99%) (ATC), Tris buffer substance (pH 7.5), bovine serum albumin (lyophilized powder, ≥96%) and ethyl 2-(4-phenoxyphenoxy)ethylcarbamate (fenoxycarb) were all obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Madrid, Spain). In order to synthesize N-GQDs, citric acid monohydrate (99.0–102.0%) and ammonia (25%) were acquired from Sigma Aldrich (Madrid, Spain) and Panreac (Barcelona, Spain), respectively. Ethanol solvent to prepare fenoxycarb stock solutions was supplied by Panreac (Barcelona, Spain). Ultrapure water (18.2MΩ) was produced through a Milli-Q water purification system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA) and used throughout all the studies.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded by a PTI QuantaMaster™ Spectrofluorometer (Photon Technology International) equipped with a 75W xenon short arc lamp and a model 814 PTM detection system. The excitation wavelength was 355nm and the emission spectrum was recorded in the 380–600nm range with a maximum peak at 440nm. Fluorescence measurements were carried out in quartz microcuvettes (10mm light path) from Hellma Analytics (Müllheim, Germany). The system control, data acquisition and data analysis were performed by FeliX32 software.
High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were obtained with a JEOL JEM 2010 electron microscope (S.C.A.I., Córdoba, Spain) operating at an acceleration voltage of 200kV.
Fourier transform mid infrared (FT-MIR) spectra were obtained on a Bruker Tensor 27FT-MIR spectrophotometer using KBr pellets.
Adjustments of pH were achieved using a pH-Meter basic 20 from Crison Instruments (Barcelona, Spain).
XPS measurements were performed on a Phoibos 150 MCD (Specs, Berlin, Germany) using an Al source.
Zeta potential of nanoparticles was obtained using a Zetasizer Nano (Malvern, Worcester, United Kingdom).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: N-GQDs were synthesized by carbonization of citric acid in presence of ammonia following the experimental protocol described by Tam et al. but including some slight modifications [20]. In short, 12mL of aqueous solution of citric acid (1mgmL−1) and 1.5mL of ammonia (25%) were mixed into a 25mL teflon lined hydrothermal synthesis autoclave reactor and then heated in an oven for 3h at 200°C. After cooling to room temperature, the N-GQD solution was adjusted at pH 9 and then dialyzed in dialysis tubes (MWCO 3.5kDa) (Sigma Aldrich, Madrid, Spain) against double-distilled water for 4h with the purpose of removing the excess of reagents and impurities. Prior to analysis, aqueous dilutions (1:25) of N-GQD synthesis solution were prepared and readjusted at pH 6 with formic acid.

Stock solutions of AChE (1mgmL−1) were prepared in 20mM Tris buffer solution (pH 7.5) containing 1mgmL−1 of bovine serum albumin (BSA) as specified by the manufacturer. AChE solutions were stored refrigerated in the dark at 2–8°C until use. ATC solutions (10mgmL−1) were prepared in ultrapure water and also stored refrigerated at 2–8°C. Stock standard solution of fenoxycarb was prepared in ethanol at a relatively high concentration (1000μM) and then, diluted with ultrapure water until obtaining the desired concentrations. The effects of organic solvent on AChE activity were found to be negligible.

The proposed analytical method was finally tested in real samples using river water collected from the Guadalquivir River (Córdoba, Spain). Samples were kept in the dark and adjusted at pH 6.0 with formic acid solution before analysis.

The sensing procedure for fenoxycarb determination was as follows: (1) different amounts of fenoxycarb were added to an aqueous solution containing AChE (100μL; 1mgmL−1) and the mixtures were incubated in the dark at 37°C for 15min; (2) an aliquot of ATC (140μL, 10mgmL−1) was added to the previous solution and then kept in the dark at 37°C for another 5min; (3) finally, 500μL of N-GQD solution (1:25 dilution) were added to each sample which was allowed to stand in the dark for 5min before fluorescence analysis (the final volume in cuvette was 1mL).

All optimization studies were conducted in absence of inhibitor, thus, variation in quenching caused by the enzymatic reaction products on the native fluorescence of N-GQDs was monitored and taken as analytical signal. On the other hand, for assays with fenoxycarb, signal was traduced into inhibition efficiency percentage, and the calibration curve was built by plotting these percentages against fenoxycarb concentrations.

The sensing approach proposed in this work for determination of fenoxycarb takes advantage of the use of fluorescent N-GQDs to assay the activity of AChE, which is highly influenced by the presence of enzyme inhibitors. The basic principle of biosensor is shown in Fig. 1 . N-GQDs exhibit a strong native fluorescence signal which results to be quenched by enzymatic reaction products (thiocholine and acetate) when AChE and ATC are both present in solution. In absence of inhibitor and under optimal conditions, the quenching effect on fluorescence of nanoparticles reaches its maximum due to the favored interaction of N-GQDs with thiocholine and acetate products. Nevertheless, the presence of fenoxycarb inhibitor, reduces the enzymatic activity of AChE for converting substrate into products, thus decreasing the quenching effect exerted on the original fluorescence of N-GQDs. This provokes a gradual recovery of fluorescence signal in an inhibitor concentration-dependent manner. Carbamate compounds are known to inhibit the activity of cholinesterases in a pseuroirreversible manner by binding to serine hydroxyl in the esteric site of enzyme [15]. Therefore, the inhibitory potential of fenoxycarb carbamate towards AChE might allow for its indirect detection through modulation of N-GQD fluorescence.

In this work, N-GQDs with circular shape and a diameter of 4.5±0.8nm were synthesized. The lattice spacing was 0.246nm. In most of the GQDs the lattice spacing is commonly smaller than 0.3nm, less than the (002) interlayer spacing of bulk graphite (0.334nm), indicating that most of the dots are actually separate graphenes [21] Fluorescence characterization revealed that the doped nanoparticles exhibited excitation and pHwavelength independent photoluminescence at 355nm, consequently, the fluorescence emission was not shifted at variable excitation wavelengths or pH changes. N-GQDs showed their maximal fluorescence emission at 440nm. The N-GQD quantum yield, calculated by using quinine sulphate as reference, resulted to be of 42%.

Nanoparticles were also characterized by FT-MIR and XPS. The infrared study demonstrated the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups, mainly carboxylic and hydroxylic groups, which confered to the doped dots with high stability and excellent water solubility. The FT-MIR spectrum of the N-GQDs showed absorption peaks corresponding to stretching vibration of CO at 1718cm−1 and the deformation of CO at 1392cm−1 in the carboxylic group. The band at 1200cm−1 was attributed to the CN stretching and that observed at 1500–1780cm−1 was assigned to the CO stretching of carboxyl groups. The stretching H-bonded vibration of the hydroxylic groups was associated with the band located at 3401cm−1. A possible contribution of the NH bonds appeared along with this band. Fig. 2 shows the results from HR-TEM and MIR characterization of N-GQDs.

XPS study was also performed for the N-GQD characterization (Fig. 3 ). The XPS survey spectrum (Fig. 3a) exhibits three peaks at around 284.6, 400, and 531.5eV, which were attributed to C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively [22]. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum of N-GQDs (Fig. 3b) shows three peaks at 284.6, 284.8 and 288.2eV, which were attributed to sp2-hybridized graphitic C (CC), sp3C from CC and CN. and OCO bonding, respectively [23]. The high-resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. 3c) reveals two peaks at 399.6 and 400eV, which were associated with pyridinic N (CNC) and pyrrolic N (N–(C3)). The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 3d) indicates three peaks at 528.2, 531.6 and 535.2eV, which were assigned to oxygen KLL auger, CO and CO species, respectively [24,25]. An elemental analysis of the dialyzed N-GQDs was also carried out with the aim of detecting the elemental composition of the N-GQD samples. Data revealed that the atomic composition of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen was 64.65%, 32.15% and 3.20% respectively.
The stability of the N-GQDs solution was contrasted with zeta potential measurements. The obtained value was −105±2.8mV, confirming the extremely good stability of the synthesized nanoparticles.

A crucial factor to take into consideration in the design of a fluorescent biosensor is the choice of the most appropriate fluorescent probe. In this context, GQDs are known to be biocompatible thanks to their high solubility in water and low toxicity. In addition, these nanoparticles exhibit extraordinary fluorescence features with the added advantage of that their fluorescence might be modulated by interaction mechanisms with other species in solution. Preliminary studies were carried out with undoped GQDs and N-GQDs in order to select those graphene nanoparticles more suitable for our sensor. Experiments with undoped nanoparticles led to more unstable fluorescent signals as well as a lower quenching effect when the enzymatic reaction took place in presence of AChE and ATC in solution. On the contrary, N-GQDs exhibited a greater and more stable original fluorescence, and interestingly, a more noticeable quenching effect was observed upon addition of both enzyme and substrate. An example of fluorescence spectrum obtained for single N-GQDs and the quenching effect caused by enzymatic reaction products is presented in Fig. 4 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of AChE and ATC concentrations
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the fact that the quenching effect on the N-GQD fluorescence was only observed when hydrolysis reaction products were in solution, optimization studies were aimed at maximizing this attenuation of the native fluorescence of nanoparticles by studying the most influential parameters in enzymatic reaction. Therefore, AChE and ATC concentrations were investigated first of all. As previously commented, AChE catalyzes the hydrolysis of ATC into thiocholine and acetate. The enzymatic activity is defined as the catalytic potential of an enzyme, expressed as units per milligram of enzyme or molecules of substrate transformed per minute per molecule of enzyme. In this specific case, the enzyme unit (U) corresponds to the amount of AChE that hydrolyzes one micromole of ATC to thiocholine and acetate per minute at pH 8 and 37°C. With the purpose of promoting a greater formation of reaction products, different AChE concentrations (expressed as number of U per mL) were tested. Briefly, the experimental protocol consisted in mixing different volumes of AChE solution with a fixed concentration of ATC (400μL; 2mgmL−1). After 30min at 37°C in the dark, 500μL of an aqueous solution of N-GQDs (1:10 dilution) were added into solution and incubated for 1h more at room temperature in the dark prior to direct analysis by fluorescence. The influence of AChE concentration on the fluorescence quenching is shown in Fig. 5 (a, b). Results revealed that higher concentrations of AChE led to a greater attenuation in the original fluorescence of N-GQDs as a consequence of the interaction of nanoparticles with a higher amount of reaction products in solution. Due to its greater influence, 27.4UmL−1 was selected as optimum concentration of AChE for subsequent studies. Next, different ATC concentrations were investigated in the assay solutions, containing the previously optimized enzyme concentration, in order to observe the variable response of N-GQD fluorescence to a wide range of substrate concentrations. Data in Fig. 5(c, d) indicate the influence of increasing ATC concentrations on the quenching effect. As observed, attenuation of fluorescence was higher for increasing amounts of substrate in solution meaning a greater formation of products resulting from enzymatic reaction. However, the increasing speed of fluorescence quenching began to slow down over the concentration of 14.16mM, and higher concentrations caused a similar quenching effect. Consequently, 14.16mM was chosen to be the optimal ATC concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH of ATC solution
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of pH was examined in order to find those experimental conditions more propitious for enzyme activity. To perform these studies, aqueous solutions of ATC were freshly prepared and adjusted at different pH values in the range of 6–9. A solution without adjusting was taken as reference for comparison. Findings showed no obvious pH changes in quenching effect by varying the pH from 6 to 9, demonstrating the absence of influence of the pH of ATC solution on N-GQD fluorescence. According to the results, the pH of ATC solution was not adjusted in experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of pH and dilution of N-GQD synthesis solution
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing mechanism proposed in this work takes advantage of the fluorescence signal of N-GQDs and its modulation, in terms of fluorescence attenuation or quenching effect, in response to the interaction of nanoparticles with other species in solution. The original fluorescence of GQDs is known to be conditioned by several variables including their concentration, oxidation and aggregation states, and solution pH among others.

There is some evidence in literature confirming that the pH of aqueous solution results to be a critical parameter in fluorescent GQD-based sensing mechanisms [26–28]. Some authors have stated that changeable pH values lead to a variation in the deprotonation degree of surface atoms of GQDs and consequently, in their fluorescent intensity [29,30]. In this work, N-GQD fluorescence was monitored by varying the pH value of N-GQD solution in the range of 5–10. Fig. 6 a shows as the observed quenching was maximum at pH 6, while higher values of pH provoked a lower effect on the original fluorescence of N-GQDs. Thus, pH 6 was chosen as optimal pH for further studies. Once the pH of N-GQD dilution was selected, different N-GQDs:H2O ratios, were assayed in the sensing procedure. The N-GQDs synthesized according to the procedure detailed in Section 2.3, had a concentration of 0.146mgmL−1. After dialysis, the synthesis solution of N-GQDs was diluted in water until obtaining the following dilutions: 1:25, 1:10, 1:5 and 1:2. Then, 500μL of each dilution, previously adjusted at pH 6, were mixed with 500μL of enzymatic assay solution (27.4UmL−1 AChE and 14.16mM ATC). Data in Fig. 6b clearly indicate a decreasing tendency of fluorescence quenching by increasing the proportion of graphene in water. These results were attributed to the fact that when the concentration of N-GQDs in solution became higher, a greater degree of approach among nanoparticles complicated their interaction with reaction products resulting in a lower quenching effect. On the other hand, more diluted nanoparticle solutions allowed for a more efficient attenuation of fluorescence by favoring their contact with hydrolysis reaction products.

In the development of the sensing system, the incubation time of AChE+ATC, and that for AChE+ATC+N-GQDs, were studied and optimized. The temperature for incubation periods between AChE and ATC was 37°C in all cases, while the interaction with N-GQDs was always conducted at room temperature.

The time of contact between enzyme (AChE) and substrate (ATC) was investigated in the first place. To this end, AChE, ATC and N-GQD solutions were used at previously fixed conditions, that is, 27.4UmL−1 AChE, 14.16mM ATC and 1:25 N-GQD dilution. The incubation times were in the range from 3 to 30min. Fig. 7 a shows the found tendency for this study. As can be seen, no remarkable differences were appreciated among all the incubation times tested. Accordingly, and reaching a compromise between rapidity and analytical signal, 5min were selected as the ideal incubation time between enzyme and substrate.

The time needed for an adequate interaction between the entire enzymatic system and the nitrogen-doped dots was finally considered. In this case, different reaction times were studied ranging from 5 to 75min. Fig. 7b depicts the obtained quenching for each reaction time studied. As can be observed, no notable differences were obtained and, for this reason, 5min were selected as optimal reaction time between N-GQDs and the entire enzymatic system.

The sensing system proposed in this work allows for determination of fenoxycarb in aqueous samples through AChE inhibition mechanisms. While N-GQDs were not directly sensitive to pesticide, fluorescence of nanoparticles resulted to be quenched in presence of enzymatic reaction products. The developed analytical method uses the potential of fenoxycarb to inhibit the AChE activity, and therefore, to modulate the quenching effect exerted by reaction products on fluorescence of N-GQDs.

Under optimal experimental conditions, the linearity of the method was then studied using ultrapure water standards containing AChE, ATC, N-GQDs and increasing concentrations of fenoxycarb (0–90μM). Inhibition efficiency (%) was chosen as an analytical signal in studies with inhibitor, and it was calculated according to this equation:
Inhibition
efficiency
( % )
=
F inhibitor
−
F
no inhibitor
F
0
−
F
no inhibitor
* 100
[ 4 ]
where Finhibitor correspond to the fluorescence intensity in presence of all components (AChE, ATC, N-GQDs and inhibitor), Fno inhibitor is the fluorescence signal for the same system but in absence of fenoxycarb, and Fo associates with the fluorescence from the mixture ATC+N-GQDs. A linear response (Y=0.50082X+3.34402; R2 =0.9941) was found in the range 6–70μM by plotting the inhibition efficiency percentage versus fenoxycarb concentration (Fig. 8 ). The limits of detection (LOD) and quantification (LOQ) were estimated to be 3.15μM and 10.51μM, respectively, based on calculation of three and ten times the standard deviation rule (LOD=3σ/S and LOQ=10σ/S, where σ is the standard deviation of y-intercept of the regression line and S is the slope of the calibration curve). The precision of the method, measured as reproducibility (% RSD), was found to be 1.76% for 3 replicates containing fenoxycarb at a concentration of 14μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of fenoxycarb in river water
ce:section.ce:para: The sensing approach was lastly tested in river water samples collected from Guadalquivir river (Córdoba, Spain). All samples were previously adjusted at pH 6 with formic acid prior to analysis. Relative recoveries were calculated as the percentage of the ratio between the found concentration of fenoxycarb applying the proposed analytical method, and the spiked concentration in river water. Acceptable values of recovery were obtained in the range 57.87–77.11% for three different levels of fenoxycarb concentration in samples, namely: 20, 40 and 60μM. The reproducibility (%RSD) of sensing system in river water was found to be in the range 2.28–6.40% (n=3). Fig. 9 shows typical fluorescence spectra obtained from analysis of real samples.

In this work, a fluorescent nanosensor based on the combination of N-GQDs as fluorescent probe and AChE as biorecognition element has been developed for determination of fenoxycarb in river water. It was experimentally demonstrated that nanoparticle fluorescence was not directly affected by inhibitor. The basis of fluorescence sensing lies on the quenching effect exerted by enzymatic hydrolysis products on N-GQD fluorescence, which might be reverted in presence of fenoxycarb, known inhibitor of AChE. Thus, increasing concentrations of fenoxycarb in the assay solution might be analyzed by the progressive recovery of nanodot fluorescence compared with the system only containing AChE+ATC+N-GQDs. A possible hypothesis that might explain the quenching behavior observed in presence of reaction products is that nanoparticles, containing oxygen-rich functional groups on its surface, could interact with sulphur atoms available in thiocholine molecules via sulphur-oxygen interactions.

This sensing approach is characterized by rapidity of analysis (about 25min), simplicity (sequential addition of components to assay solution), excellent reproducibility in real samples (%RSD 2.83), and the absence of other intermediary species. While the limit of detection of the designed method is in accordance with literature, the recovery values obtained from analysis of spiked river water were only acceptable (57.87–77.11%). However, this analytical approach has the added advantage of using highly fluorescent nanodots synthesized by a very simple synthetic route without requirements of enzymatic immobilization steps. Besides, the developed sensing strategy constitutes an appropriate analytical tool for determination of AChE inhibitors and could be applied to other scenarios involving different enzymes, substrates and inhibitors.
The authors have declared no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors want to thank to the Spanish Ministry of Economy and Competitiveness for financial support (project CTQ2014-52939R). The authors also want to thanks to the Research Support Central Service (SCAI) and to the Fine Chemistry Institute of the University of Córdoba for technical support.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Semiconductor and carbon-based fluorescent nanodots: the need for consistency
Enhanced graphene quantum dot fluorescence nanosensor for highly sensitive acetylcholinesterase assay and inhibitor screening
Graphene quantum dots in analytical science
Upconversion nanoparticle-based fluorescence resonance energy transfer assay for organophosphorus pesticides
Mixture toxicity of wood preservative products in the fish embryo toxicity test
Population growth rate responses of Ceriodaphnia dubia to ternary mixtures of specific acting chemicals: pharmacological versus ecotoxicological modes of action
Timing matters: sensitivity of Daphnia magna dormant eggs to fenoxycarb exposure depends on embryonic developmental stage
Determination of the insecticide fenoxycarb in apple leaf samples by an enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay
Development of high-performance liquid chromatography and non-aqueous capillary electrophoresis methods for the determination of fenoxycarb residues in wheat samples
An on-line SPE-HPLC method for effective sample preconcentration and determination of fenoxycarb and cis, trans-permethrin in surface waters
Determination of sixteen pesticides in peppers using high-performance liquid chromatography/mass spectrometry
Magnetic octadecyl-based matrix solid-phase dispersion coupled with gas chromatography with mass spectrometry in a proof-of-concept determination of multi-class pesticide residues in carrots

A simple multi-residue method for the determination of pesticides in fruits and vegetables using a methanolic extraction and ultra-high-performance liquid chromatography–tandem mass spectrometry: optimization and extension of scope
Optical detection of organophosphorus compounds based on Mn-doped ZnSe d-dot enzymatic catalytic sensor
Cholinesterases in biorecognition and biosensors construction: a review
Acetylcholinesterase based dipsticks with indoxylacetate as a substrate for assay of organophosphates and carbamates
Modulated dye retention for the signal-on fluorometric determination of acetylcholinesterase inhibitor
Colorimetric and fluorometric assays for acetylcholinesterase and its inhibitors screening based on a fluorescein derivate
A fluorometric assay for acetylcholinesterase activity and inhibitor screening with carbon quantum dots
One-pot synthesis of N-doped graphene quantum dots as a fluorescent sensing platform for Fe3+ ions detection
Facile synthesis of water-soluble, highly fluorescent graphene quantum dots as a robust biological label for stem cells
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots@SiO2 nanoparticles as electrochemiluminescence and fluorescence signal indicators for magnetically controlled aptasensor with dual detection channels
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots with oxygen-rich functional groups
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots-based fluorescent probe for the sensitive turn-on detection of glutathione and its cellular imaging
Graphene quantum dots as sensor for phenols in olive oil
Highly sensitive enzymatic determination of urea based on the pH-dependence of the fluorescence of graphene quantum dots
Nitrogen- doped graphene quantum dots: turn-off fluorescent probe for detection of Ag+ ions
Graphenes in chemical sensors and biosensors
One-pot green synthesis of oxygen-rich nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots and their potential application in pH-sensitive photoluminescence and detection of mercury (II) ions

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Encarnación Caballero-Díaz studied a Bacheloŕs degree in Environmental Sciences at University of Córdoba (Spain) from 2003 to 2008 achieving the extraordinary end-of-studies award. Subsequently, she did a Master and Doctorate in Advanced Fine Chemistry at the same University, joining the staff of the Analytical Chemistry Department in 2009. Her research line is focused on the Analytical Nanoscience and Nanotechnology field, specifically, in the development of new analytical methods based on the use of nanoparticles and in nanotoxicity studies. She performed a predoctoral research stay at Philipps-Universität Marburg (Germany) in 2011 under supervision of Professor Wolfgang Parak. Recently, she has completed a short postdoctoral stay in the Institute of Chemical Technologies and Analytics of Vienna University with Professor Bernhard Lendl.

Sandra Benítez-Martínez obtained the Environmental Sciences degree in 2007 at the University of Córdoba. In order to expand her scientific knowledge she attended a scientific Post degree Master in Advanced Fine Chemistry carrying out a Master Project focused in using graphene nanosheets as pseudostationary phase in electrokinetic chromatography (2011). Later, she started her Doctorate studies focused in the use of graphene and graphene derivates as tools for the improvement of the analytical process and received her Ph.D degree in 2015. Nowadays she has a postdoctoral fellow in the Analytical Chemistry Department of the University of Córdoba and her actual research interests lie in the study about nano-enzymatic systems.

Miguel Valcárcel has been Full Professor of Analytical Chemistry at the University of Córdoba since 1976 being his master research lines analytical nanoscience and nanotechnology and automatization/miniaturization/quality of (bio)chemical measurement processes. He is the author and co-author of more than 900 scientific articles, 8 monographs, 10 textbooks and 20 chapters of books. He has been the coordinator of 25 Spanish scientific research projects and 14 of international nature, and of 12 contracts with private firms, and has promoted a spin-off devoted to nanotechnology. Professor Valcárcel has been co-supervisor of 78 doctoral theses and invited lecturer at 70 international meetings. He is the recipient of scientific national (e.g. Award in Chemistry in Spain, 2005) and international (e.g. Robert Boyle Medal of the RSC, 2004) prizes as well as Doctor Honoris Causa by the University of Valencia (Spain, 2010).
